1: They set out from Elim, and all the congregation of the people of Israel came to the wilderness of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai: The wilderness of Sin (midbar Sin) should not be confused with the Sinai peninsula name — Sin is likely derived from the Sumero-Akkadian moon-god Sin (Nanna), whose worship was practiced at the Egyptian mining and trading outpost of Serabit el-Khadim in the Sinai. The route from Elim (12 springs, 70 palms) through the wilderness of Sin toward Sinai follows the western Sinai coastal track. The date — the fifteenth of the second month — is exactly one month after the Passover/Exodus, giving the manna miracle a precise chronological anchor.
2: And the whole congregation of the people of Israel grumbled (vayyillonu) against Moses and Aaron in the wilderness: The wilderness complaint-cycle (murmuring) is a consistent structural feature of the wilderness narrative that is theologically analyzed in Ps 78, 95, 106, and 1 Corinthians 10:6-10. The technical verb lun (to grumble/murmur) appears most frequently in Exodus and Numbers. Ancient Near Eastern parallels for wilderness-survival complaints exist in Egyptian travel literature (the Sinuhe story, c. 1900 BCE, describes the hardships of desert travel) but the structured theological pattern of complaint-provision-ingratitude is unique to Israel's covenant relationship with YHWH.
3: 'We sat by the meat pots (sirot habasar) and ate bread to the full. For you have brought us out into this wilderness to kill this whole assembly with hunger.' The 'meat pots of Egypt' (sirot habasar) have become proverbial for the deceptive attractiveness of slavery's material provisions. Archaeologically, the diet of Egyptian laborers (including foreign slave-labor) is attested in ancient Egyptian administrative records: daily rations of bread, beer, vegetables, and occasional fish and meat were issued to workers on large state projects. The memory of adequate nutrition in Egypt (even in servitude) vs. the uncertainty of the wilderness is a genuine psychological tension.
4: 'Behold, I am about to rain bread (lechem) from heaven for you, and the people shall go out and gather a day's portion every day, that I may test them (anassenu), whether they will walk in my law or not.' The test (nassah) structure — provision given with conditions attached to reveal the people's heart — is the standard ANE framework for covenant loyalty trials. The manna is simultaneously provision and test: daily dependence built into the logistics (gather only one day's portion). The 'bread from heaven' (lechem min hashamayim) formula becomes the OT template for the eschatological messianic feast; Jesus cites it explicitly (John 6:31-35) when identifying himself as the true bread from heaven.
5: 'On the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it will be twice as much as they gather daily.' The double portion on the sixth day is the first Sabbath instruction in the post-Exodus narrative — the Sabbath principle is introduced through the manna logistics before it is formally legislated at Sinai (Exodus 20). The manna narrative is the proto-legislation for the Sabbath: Israel learns the rest-pattern through food provision before the commandment is given. The double-gathering on day six (and the non-availability on day seven) is the material form of the commandment.
6: So Moses and Aaron said to all the people of Israel, 'At evening you shall know that it was YHWH who brought you out of the land of Egypt.' The evening-morning structure of the provision (quail at evening, manna in the morning) mirrors the creation-week's evening-morning pattern (Gen 1). The provision is both a daily re-creation and a daily reminder of the Exodus — 'you shall know (yeda'tem) that YHWH brought you out': the manna is an epistemological event, producing covenant knowledge (da'at) through daily experience.
7: 'And in the morning you shall see the glory (k'vod) of YHWH, because he has heard your grumbling against YHWH. For what are we, that you grumble against us?' The glory of YHWH (kavod YHWH) appears in the cloud (Exodus 16:10) as the visible manifestation of the divine presence. The logic of Moses's rebuke: grumbling against Moses and Aaron is grumbling against YHWH, because they are YHWH's agents. The delegation of divine authority creates a corresponding accountability — rejecting the representative is rejecting the principal. This principle structures all subsequent prophetic authority claims.
8: 'When YHWH gives you in the evening meat to eat and in the morning bread to the full, because YHWH has heard your grumbling that you grumble against him — what are we? Your grumbling is not against us but against YHWH.' The paired provision (meat/evening + bread/morning) as a direct divine response to the complaint 'we sat by the meat pots' (Exodus 16:3). YHWH does not rebuke the hunger complaint per se but provides exactly what was requested while simultaneously transforming the context: the provision comes from YHWH, not Egypt. The meat pots of Egypt are replaced by quail from YHWH.
9: Then Moses said to Aaron, 'Say to the whole congregation of the people of Israel, Come near before YHWH, for he has heard your grumbling.' The formal assembly (qahal) before YHWH — the congregation draws near not for punishment but for provision. The divine response to the wilderness complaint is grace, not wrath (at this stage in the wilderness journey). The pattern changes later (Num 11) when YHWH's anger is kindled by the same complaint after Sinai — the covenant context changes the moral weight of ingratitude.
10: And as soon as Aaron spoke to the whole congregation of the people of Israel, they looked toward the wilderness, and behold, the glory of YHWH appeared in the cloud: The glory in the cloud (kavod YHWH b'anan) is the visible theophanic manifestation that will later settle on Sinai (Exodus 24:16) and fill the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34-35). The appearance of kavod is here in response to the people's complaint — the divine response to human need comes through the manifestation of divine presence. The cloud that guided Israel (Exodus 13:21-22) is the same cloud in which the glory dwells.
11: And YHWH said to Moses, 'I have heard the grumbling of the people of Israel.' YHWH's direct acknowledgment that he heard (shama'ti et-t'lunot) the grumbling is remarkable: the same divine hearing that responded to the cry of oppression in Egypt (Exodus 2:24; Exodus 3:7) now responds to the grumbling of the wilderness. YHWH's responsiveness to Israel's needs — even their faithless complaints — is a consistent feature of the wilderness relationship that structures subsequent prophetic appeals to the Exodus tradition.
12: 'At twilight you shall eat meat, and in the morning you shall be filled with bread. Then you shall know that I am YHWH your God.' The da'at-YHWH formula ('you shall know that I am YHWH your God') that drove the plague narrative now structures the wilderness provision. The plagues made Egypt know YHWH by judgment; the manna makes Israel know YHWH by provision. The same epistemological purpose (knowledge of YHWH) is achieved through opposite means for opposite audiences.
13: In the evening quail (s'lav) came up and covered the camp, and in the morning dew lay around the camp: S'lav = quail (Coturnix coturnix, the common quail). Quail migrate annually across the Sinai Peninsula from Africa to Europe in the spring (March-April) and return in the autumn. They migrate at night in enormous flocks and frequently land exhausted near the ground, making them easy to catch in large numbers. The quail provision is seasonally and geographically plausible in the Sinai setting — but the timing and location of the provision at the precise moment of Israel's need is the miraculous element. The Sinai quail-migration tradition is attested in ancient Egyptian texts.
14: And when the dew had gone up, there was on the face of the wilderness a fine, flake-like thing, fine as frost on the ground: The manna's physical description — fine (daq), flake-like (m'chuspas), like hoarfrost — has invited comparison with natural substances. The leading natural candidate is the secretion of the tamarisk scale insect (Trabutina mannipara), which exudes a honey-like substance on tamarisk trees in the Sinai that appears as small, white, crystalline flakes in the morning; Bedouin collect and use it today. However, the manna's miraculous features (double on day six, no maggots on sabbath, none on sabbath, melting in the noon heat) substantially exceed what any natural substance does, indicating that natural parallels inform the description but do not explain the phenomenon.
15: When the people of Israel saw it, they said to one another, 'What is it (man hu')?' For they did not know what it was. Man hu' (what is it?) = the etymological explanation for the name 'manna' (man); the word is probably related to the Egyptian 'mannu' or Semitic 'manna' (a secretion from plants or insects). The people name the substance by their ignorance of it — manna = the anonymous food, identified only by its divine origin and the question it provokes. Jesus's 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35) takes up this etymology: he identifies himself as the answer to 'man hu' — the one who truly is the divine provision.
16: 'This is what YHWH has commanded: Gather of it, each one of you, as much as he can eat. You shall each take an omer (omer) according to the number of the persons that each of you has in his tent.' An omer (from the same root as 'sheaf' — the omer offering of Leviticus 23:10-14) = 1/10 of an ephah (approximately 2 liters). The per-person measuring system distributes the manna provision equitably: no household takes more than its proportional share. The omer measure will later be the unit for the firstfruits offering — the manna teaches Israel the economics of provision before they enter the agricultural land where the same principle will govern their harvest offering.
17: And the people of Israel did so. They gathered, some more, some less. The brief notation of variety in gathering (who took more and who took less) sets up the miraculous equalization of Exodus 16:18 — despite the difference in gathering, everyone had exactly what they needed. The distributive miracle (much-gathered = right amount; little-gathered = right amount) is a parable of divine provision that transcends human economy: in the wilderness economy of grace, the measure of effort does not determine the measure of provision.
18: But when they measured it with an omer, whoever gathered much had nothing left over, and whoever gathered little had no lack. Each of them gathered as much as he could eat. Paul cites this verse in 2 Cor 8:15 to support the principle of economic leveling in the Jerusalem collection: 'as it is written, Whoever gathered much had nothing left over, and whoever gathered little had no lack.' The manna economy becomes Paul's model for Christian generosity — the surplus of some and the need of others are resolved by mutual sharing that produces equality (isotēs).
19: And Moses said to them, 'Let no one leave any of it over till morning.' The command against hoarding manna overnight is the material form of daily dependence on YHWH. Hoarding food is the natural human response to scarcity anxiety — the command against it forces a daily faith-act: trusting that tomorrow's provision will come. The prohibition has exact structural parallels in ANE daily-ration systems (Egyptian and Mesopotamian administrative records show that daily rations were not transferable to the next day).
20: But they did not listen to Moses. Some left part of it till morning, and it bred worms (va'yaleer tolaim) and stank. And Moses was angry with them. Tolaim = worms/maggots (the decomposition that natural food undergoes). The overnight spoilage is the natural physical consequence of trying to secure today's provision against tomorrow's uncertainty. The worms and stench are YHWH's enforced lesson in non-hoarding: you cannot save up grace for tomorrow. The manna-economy runs entirely on daily trust.
21: Morning by morning they gathered it, each as much as he could eat; but when the sun grew hot, it melted: The morning gathering before the heat of the day is consistent with the Sinai climate — by mid-morning, temperatures rise sharply and anything left on the ground would indeed melt or dry up. The manna's time-sensitive character (gather before the sun gets hot) reinforces the daily urgency: provision is available but not indefinitely, requiring an active daily response.
22: On the sixth day they gathered twice as much bread, two omers each. And when all the leaders of the congregation came and told Moses: The double-gathering on day six is the community's experiential discovery of the Sabbath pattern before they know what to call it. The leaders' report to Moses ('we gathered double today') is the occasion for Moses to explain what YHWH has already built into the manna-provision system. The Sabbath is discovered inductively through the provision before it is commanded deductively at Sinai — a pedagogical method consistent with ANE treaty ratification processes where covenant obligations are experienced before formally stated.
23: He said to them, 'This is what YHWH has commanded: Tomorrow is a day of solemn rest (shabbaton), a holy sabbath to YHWH.' The first explicit use of shabbaton (intensive/solemn rest, from shavat = to cease) in the Bible. The word's first appearance names the coming reality that the manna-provision has been silently teaching. Shabbaton is a stronger form than simply shabbat; it occurs primarily in the Pentateuch for the most sacred rest occasions (Leviticus 16:31 = the Day of Atonement; Leviticus 23:24 = Rosh Hashana). The manna's sabbath-provision inaugurates the week as a theological unit of time before the formal Decalogue.
24: So they laid it aside till the morning, as Moses commanded them, and it did not stink, and there were no worms in it: The Sabbath exception: the manna stored overnight on the sixth day does not spoil — no stench, no maggots. The miraculous preservation is the physical sign that the Sabbath rest is genuinely YHWH-commanded and YHWH-protected. The same food that rots when hoarded on other days stays fresh when stored for the Sabbath. The physical miracle validates the theological principle: Sabbath rest is not laziness but obedience.
25: Moses said, 'Eat it today, for today is a sabbath to YHWH; today you will not find it in the field.' The command to eat the stored manna on the Sabbath confirms that the Sabbath is a day of provision and rest, not of fasting. The Sabbath is not characterized by lack but by sufficiency — what was gathered and stored on day six is enough for day seven. The provision is already there before the rest begins; the work of provision is complete before the rest is entered.
26: 'Six days you shall gather it, but on the seventh day, which is a sabbath, there will be none.' The absolute statement 'there will be none' (lo yihyeh bo) on the sabbath makes the sabbath manna-absence a structural feature of creation's weekly pattern, not a temporary exception. The seven-day week is here given its first explicit theological rationale through lived experience: six days of active provision, one day of divinely-prepared rest.
27: On the seventh day some of the people went out to gather, but they found nothing: The test is immediate — some people disbelieve the announced sabbath-absence and go to gather. They find nothing. The Sabbath is not merely a divine suggestion; it is a feature of created reality. The disobedience is not punished directly (unlike later sabbath violations in Numbers 15:32-36) because the context is the initial introduction of the sabbath principle. The finding-nothing is itself the correction.
28: And YHWH said to Moses, 'How long will you refuse (me'antem) to keep my commandments and my laws?' YHWH's rebuke of the manna-gatherers on the Sabbath — 'how long will you refuse?' — uses the same verb (me'aneyn = refuse, from ana' = to restrain/refuse) that Moses used to warn Pharaoh before the plagues. The irony: Israel in the wilderness refuses YHWH's commandments just as Pharaoh refused YHWH's demands. The wilderness generation mirrors Pharaoh's obduracy at smaller scale.
29: 'See! YHWH has given you the sabbath; therefore on the sixth day he gives you bread for two days. Remain each of you in his place; let no one go out of his place on the seventh day.' 'YHWH has given you the sabbath (natan lachem et-ha-shabbat)' — the Sabbath is a gift (natan), not merely a restriction. The double provision on day six is the material gift that makes the Sabbath rest possible. This verse is the only place in the Hebrew Bible where the Sabbath is explicitly described as a divine gift. The 'remain in your place' instruction anticipates later Sabbath legislation about travel limits.
30: So the people rested on the seventh day: The brief notation of compliance — they rested (va'yishbetu) — uses the sabbath verb (shavat) for the people's action. The first Sabbath rest observed by Israel is not the seventh day of creation (which was YHWH's rest) but this seventh day in the wilderness of Sin. The people participate in the rest-pattern that creation embeds in time by obeying the manna's logic.
31: Now the house of Israel called its name manna (man). It was like coriander seed (gad), white, and the taste of it was like wafers made with honey: The official naming of manna by Israel (beit Yisrael) confirms the folk etymology of Exodus 16:15 (man hu' = what is it?). Gad = coriander seed (Coriandrum sativum); the comparison establishes the manna's size and shape (small, round, white). The taste like 'wafers in honey' (tzapichit bidvash) is the first biblical food description — the food of grace tastes sweet. Numbers 11:8 adds another flavor comparison (taste like cakes baked with oil), suggesting different preparations produced different taste experiences.
32: Moses said, 'This is what YHWH has commanded: Let an omer of it be kept throughout your generations, so that they may see the bread with which I fed you in the wilderness, when I brought you out of the land of Egypt.' The preservation of an omer of manna as a memorial (l'dor dorot = throughout your generations) is the first object designated for permanent preservation in Israel's covenant memory. The manna-jar anticipates the ark of the covenant as the repository of covenant signs: tablets of the law, Aaron's rod, and the manna-jar (Hebrews 9:4) are the three objects preserved in the ark. Each represents a dimension of the wilderness covenant: law (tablets), priestly authority (Aaron's rod), and divine provision (manna).
33: And Moses said to Aaron, 'Take a jar (tzintznent) and put an omer of manna in it, and place it before YHWH to be kept throughout your generations.' Tzintznenet = a jar/flask (a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible; possibly a loanword from Egyptian or Canaanite). The jar is placed 'before YHWH' (lifnei YHWH) — in the context of the wilderness, this means before the testimony/covenant documents; later, Hebrews 9:4 specifies the jar was inside the ark. The preservation of the manna creates a tangible link between the wilderness generation and all future generations who would see the jar.
34: As YHWH commanded Moses, so Aaron placed it before the Testimony to be kept: The Testimony (ha'edut) refers to the tablets of the covenant that will be given at Sinai — the manna-jar is placed before documents that don't yet exist, anticipating the tabernacle's construction. The narrative anticipates the completed sanctuary as the natural resting place for the manna memorial, retroactively locating Aaron's action in its ultimate institutional context.
35: The people of Israel ate the manna forty years (arba'im shanah), till they came to a habitable land. They ate the manna till they came to the border of the land of Canaan: The forty years of manna is one of the most specific numerical claims in the Exodus-wilderness narrative. Forty years of daily miraculous provision is an extraordinary claim; the reference to it ending when Israel reached Canaan is confirmed in Joshua 5:12 ('the manna ceased on the day after the Passover, when they ate of the produce of the land'). The continuity of provision from the wilderness of Sin through all forty years frames the manna as the signature of YHWH's covenant faithfulness for the entire wilderness period.
36: An omer is the tenth part of an ephah: The final verse supplies a units-conversion note for readers unfamiliar with the omer measure. An ephah = approximately 20-22 liters; an omer = 1/10 of an ephah = approximately 2 liters. This is the only explicit conversion formula for the omer in the OT; Leviticus 23:13 and Numbers 28:5 use the omer but don't define it. The note is a scribal gloss explaining an older measure to later readers — evidence of the text's reception history within the Hebrew scribal tradition.
